seoTitle: Extract Multiple Web Pages With Power Automate
metaDescription: Build a Power Automate Desktop flow that loops through multiple URLs, extracts webpage data automatically, and saves the results to Excel or CSV.
intro: Manually copying data from dozens of web pages is slow and error-prone, and Power Automate Desktop can take over that work without you needing to write code. This guide covers building a desktop flow that opens multiple pages, extracts the data you need, and saves it somewhere useful like Excel or CSV.

steps.heading: Create a desktop flow
steps.body: Start a new flow in Power Automate Desktop, which gives you the building blocks for browser automation and data extraction without advanced programming.

steps.heading: Open and loop through multiple web pages
steps.body: Set up actions that open a browser, navigate through a list of URLs, and repeat the same extraction steps automatically for each page.

steps.heading: Extract data from each page
steps.body: Use the extraction actions to pull text, tables, links, or other elements from every page in the loop and store the results in variables.

steps.heading: Save results to Excel or CSV
steps.body: Write the collected data out to Excel or CSV so it's ready for review, reporting, or further processing once the flow finishes running.

faq.question: Do I need coding skills to extract web data with Power Automate?
faq.answer: No, Power Automate Desktop is built for web automation and data extraction without requiring advanced programming knowledge.

faq.question: Can Power Automate handle pagination and dynamic content?
faq.answer: Yes, a properly built desktop flow can handle pagination and dynamic web content as part of the browser automation steps.

faq.question: Where does the extracted data get saved?
faq.answer: You can export the collected data to Excel, CSV, or another destination of your choice once the flow completes extraction.

faq.question: What if a page fails to load during the flow?
faq.answer: Building error handling into your flow helps it recover from failed page loads so the whole automation doesn't stop unexpectedly.
